rationale_for_baseline_tier: Large-volume bright-red rectal bleeding with sudden severe abdominal pain, near-syncope, profound weakness and pallor in an older vasculopath after an antihypertensive-induced hypotensive episode — a gastrointestinal haemorrhage with haemodynamic shock. These are patient-reportable, time-critical emergency features (massive PR bleeding, near-collapse, signs of shock), so the T4/T5 telehealth ceiling is legitimately breached: T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is the only correct tier. The AI cannot palpate the abdomen, do a DRE, measure BP, obtain lactate or image the bowel — but it does not need to in order to recognise the emergency and escalate. Keep the patient flat with legs raised, nil by mouth, and withhold antihypertensives pending hospital resuscitation, imaging and surgical review.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Continued or increasing bright-red rectal bleeding, or worsening weakness/faintness before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Stay on the line with 000 and report deterioration immediately
escalation_edges.rationale: Ongoing haemorrhage deepens the shock; paramedics must know to prioritise and prepare fluid resuscitation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the bleeding continues or he gets weaker or more faint, tell the 000 dispatcher straight away so they upgrade the priority — keep him flat with his legs raised.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Loss of consciousness, the abdomen suddenly becoming hard and board-like, or he stops breathing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Update 000 immediately; begin CPR if he stops breathing and you are able
escalation_edges.rationale: Collapse signals decompensated shock, and a rigid abdomen signals perforation/peritonism — both are immediately life-threatening and change paramedic urgency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he passes out, his tummy goes hard like a board, or he stops breathing — tell 000 immediately, lay him flat with legs raised, and start CPR if he stops breathing and you know how.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Established cardiovascular disease (prior CABG) with iatrogenic hypotension from an antihypertensive double dose
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Explicitly advise withholding all further blood-pressure medication until hospital review; the vasculopath background and low-flow trigger make the colonic ischaemia and shock more dangerous and lower the threshold for immediate 000 escalation.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older adult (~72) with reduced physiological reserve
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Older patients decompensate faster from GI blood loss; do not be reassured by an initially maintained conscious state — escalate now and keep him flat and nil by mouth.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them he is passing a lot of bright-red blood and is faint and weak — a serious internal bleed. Keep him lying flat on the floor with his legs raised and completely still; do not let him stand or walk to the toilet. Give him nothing to eat or drink in case he needs an emergency operation. Do not give any more blood-pressure tablets. Unlock the front door and stay with him until the paramedics arrive.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or the GP.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary with the confirmed diagnosis, imaging/colonoscopy findings and a medication-review plan for the antihypertensives — take these to the GP and cardiologist.
